> Setting $\frac{\partial E}{\partial u} = 0$ and $\frac{\partial E}{\partial v} = 0$ yields the Euler–Lagrange equations:
> $$I_x(I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda \nabla^2 u = 0$$
> $$I_y(I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda \nabla^2 v = 0$$
> These are solved iteratively (e.g., Gauss–Seidel relaxation).

## 2.5 Discontinuity-Preserving Regularization

Quadratic smoothness penalties **over-smooth** motion boundaries (e.g., where one object occludes another). To preserve discontinuities, replace the quadratic with a **robust function** $\psi(\cdot)$:
$$E_{\text{smooth}} = \iint \psi\left(\left\|\nabla u\right\|^2 + \left\|\nabla v\right\|^2\right) dx\, dy$$
Common choices:
- **$L_1$ (total variation)**: $\psi(s) = \sqrt{s + \epsilon}$ — preserves sharp edges.
- **Huber function**: quadratic near zero, linear elsewhere.
- **Lorentzian**: $\psi(s) = \log\left(1 + \tfrac{s}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GLCM element | $P(i,j) = \dfrac{N(i,j)}{\sum_{i,j} N(i,j)}$ | Normalised joint probability |
| 2 | GLCM Energy (Angular Second Moment) | $E = \sum_{i,j} P(i,j)^2$ | Dimensionless; high = uniform texture |
| 3 | GLCM Contrast | $C = \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ | Grey-level$^2$ |
| 4 | GLCM Homogeneity | $H = \sum_{i,j} \dfrac{P(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^2}$ | Dimensionless; high = smooth |
| 5 | GLCM Entropy | $S = -\sum_{i,j} P(i,j) \log P(i,j)$ | Bits |
| 6 | LBP code | $\sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(i_p - i_c) 2^p$ | Integer in $[0, 2^P-1]$ |
| 7 | Optical Flow Constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Pixels/frame |
| 8 | Horn–Schunck functional | $E = \iint \left[(I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \lambda (\|\nabla u\|^2 + \|\nabla v\|^2)\right] dx\, dy$ | Energy in arbitrary units |
| 9 | Laplacian (discretised) | $\nabla^2 u \approx \bar{u} - u$ | $\bar{u}$ = local mean |
| 10 | Iterative update for $u$ | $u^{n+1} = \bar{u}^n - \dfrac{I_x (I_x \bar{u}^n + I_y \bar{v}^n + I_t)}{\lambda + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | Per pixel |
| 11 | Occluding contour condition | $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$ | Dot product of normal and view |
| 12 | 2D Gabor filter | $g(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma_x\sigma_y} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x^2}{\sigma_x^2}+\frac{y^2}{\sigma_y^2}\right)} e^{j 2\pi f_0 x}$ | Frequency $f_0$ in cycles/pixel |
| 13 | Tikhonov regularisation | $\min_u \|Au - b\|^2 + \lambda \|Lu\|^2$ | $L$ = regularisation operator |
| 14 | Total Variation penalty | $\text{TV}(u) = \int \|\nabla u\| \, dx\, dy$ | Discontinuity-preserving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units:** The "energy" $E$ in regularization is a scalar objective — it has no physical unit; values are relative and only meaningful for comparison between candidate solutions.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Texture** is used in automated inspection (defect detection on metal surfaces), satellite image classification (land-cover mapping), and medical imaging (tumour characterisation on MRI/CT).
- **Occluding contours** are the foundation of *visual hull* reconstruction in 3D scanning systems (e.g., multi-camera silhouette-based modelling used in cultural heritage preservation).
- **Optical flow** powers video stabilisation in consumer cameras, autonomous vehicle ego-motion estimation, action recognition in surveillance, and motion-based compression (MPEG).
- **Regularization** is the conceptual backbone of nearly every modern inverse-problem solver — denoising, deblurring, super-resolution, depth-from-stereo, and even training deep neural networks (weight decay = Tikhonov regularisation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Horn–Schunck Euler–Lagrange Equations

We start from the Horn–Schunck functional:
$$E(u, v) = \iint_{\Omega} \left[ \left( I_x u + I_y v + I_t \right)^2 + \lambda \left( u_x^2 + u_y^2 + v_x^2 + v_y^2 \right) \right] dx\, dy$$

**Step 1 — Write the integrand as a Lagrangian density.**  
Let $F(u, v, u_x, u_y, v_x, v_y) = (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \lambda (u_x^2 + u_y^2 + v_x^2 + v_y^2)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation for $u$:**  
$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial u} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_y}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\frac{\partial F}{\partial u}$:**  
$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial u} = 2(I_x u + I_y v + I_t) \cdot I_x$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_x}$:**  
$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_x} = 2\lambda u_x$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $x$ and $y$:**  
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(2\lambda u_x\right) = 2\lambda u_{xx}, \qquad \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(2\lambda u_y\right) = 2\lambda u_{yy}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the Euler–Lagrange equation:**  
$$2 I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - 2\lambda (u_{xx} + u_{yy}) = 0$$

**Step 7 — Divide by 2 and recognise the Laplacian:**  
$$I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda \nabla^2 u = 0$$

**Step 8 — By symmetry (swap $u \leftrightarrow v$):**  
$$I_y (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda \nabla^2 v = 0$$

These are the two coupled Euler–Lagrange equations of the Horn–Schunck model. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discretised Iterative Update for $u$ and $v$

**Step 1 — Replace the Laplacian by the discrete approximation** $\nabla^2 u \approx \bar{u} - u$, where $\bar{u}$ is the local spatial average of $u$ over a 4-neighbourhood:
$$\bar{u}_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substituting into the Euler–Lagrange equation for $u$:**  
$$I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda (\bar{u} - u) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand and group terms in $u$:**  
$$(I_x^2 + \lambda) u + I_x I_y v + I_x I_t - \lambda \bar{u} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $u$:**  
$$u = \bar{u} - \frac{I_x (I_x \bar{u} + I_y \bar{v} + I_t)}{\lambda + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$$

**Step 5 — This is a fixed-point equation. Use Gauss–Seidel iteration:**  
$$u^{n+1} = \bar{u}^n - \frac{I_x (I_x \bar{u}^n + I_y \bar{v}^n + I_t)}{\lambda + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$$
$$v^{n+1} = \bar{v}^n - \frac{I_y (I_x \bar{u}^n + I_y \bar{v}^n + I_t)}{\lambda + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$$

**Step 6 — Convergence:** Iterate until $\vert u^{n+1} - u^n \vert < \epsilon$ for all pixels. The process converges for any $\lambda > 0$ (the matrix is strictly diagonally dominant). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LBP Thresholding Function

**Step 1 — Define the centre pixel intensity** $i_c = I(x_c, y_c)$ and a circular neighbourhood of $P$ samples at radius $R$:
$$i_p = I\left(x_c + R\cos\frac{2\pi p}{P}, \, y_c + R\sin\frac{2\pi p}{P}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the difference signal** $d_p = i_p - i_c$.

**Step 3 — Apply the signum (step) function:**  
$$s(d_p) = \begin{cases} 1, & d_p \ge 0 \\ 0, & d_p < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Weight each bit by $2^p$ and sum:**  
$$\text{LBP}_{P,R} = \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(i_p - i_c) \cdot 2^p$$

**Step 5 — The output is an integer in $[0, 2^P - 1]$** that encodes the local micro-pattern of the texture. A histogram of these codes over a region is the final texture descriptor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For $P = 8$ neighbours, if the binary sequence (read clockwise from top) is `11001011`, then:
> $$\text{LBP} = 1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 2 + 0\cdot 4 + 0\cdot 8 + 1\cdot 16 + 0\cdot 32 + 1\cdot 64 + 1\cdot 128 = 211$$
> $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — GLCM Contrast and Homogeneity

**Step 1 — Start from the normalised GLCM** $P(i, j)$ for a chosen offset $(d, \theta)$.

**Step 2 — Contrast** is defined as the second-moment of the intensity difference weighted by its probability:
$$C = \sum_{i=0}^{G-1} \sum_{j=0}^{G-1} (i - j)^2 \, P(i, j)$$

**Step 3 — If the texture is uniform (diagonal GLCM, $i = j$), then $(i - j)^2 = 0$ and $C = 0$**.

**Step 4 — If the GLCM has off-diagonal mass (sharp intensity transitions), $C$ is large**.

**Step 5 — Homogeneity** is defined as the inverse-distance-weighted sum:
$$H = \sum_{i,j} \frac{P(i, j)}{1 + (i - j)^2}$$

**Step 6 — For a diagonal GLCM, $H = \sum P(i,i)/(1+0) = 1$ (maximum)**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Principle | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GLCM (Statistical) | Joint distribution of grey levels | Captures micro-texture; well-established | Slow for large $G$; sensitive to rotation | Periodic & random textures |
| LBP (Statistical) | Thresholded local neighbourhood | Fast; rotation-invariant; simple | Loses magnitude info | Real-time, illumination-robust |
| Gabor (Spectral) | Band-pass filtering | Multi-scale/orientation; mimics V1 cortex | Many parameters; expensive | Oriented, periodic patterns |
| Markov Random Fields | Stochastic spatial model | Captures local dependencies | Parameter estimation hard | Synthesising & segmenting textures |
| Structural | Arrangement of texture primitives | Interpretable | Needs primitive detection | Highly regular textures (bricks) |
| Horn–Schunck (Optical Flow) | Regularised variational | Dense, smooth flow | Over-smooths motion boundaries | Scenes with smooth motion |
| Lucas–Kanade (Optical Flow) | Local least-squares | Fast; preserves discontinuities | Sparse; needs corners | Sparse feature tracking |
| Total Variation (Regularisation) | $L_1$ gradient penalty | Preserves edges | Non-smooth optimisation | Discontinuous motion fields |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Texture Analysis Pipeline (GLCM + LBP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Convert to Greyscale]
    B --> C{Choose Texture Method}
    C --> D1[GLCM Path]
    C --> D2[LBP Path]
    C --> D3[Gabor Path]
    D1 --> E1[Quantise to G Levels]
    E1 --> F1[Compute Co-occurrence Matrix]
    F1 --> G1[Extract Haralick Features]
    G1 --> H1[Contrast]
    G1 --> H2[Energy]
    G1 --> H3[Homogeneity]
    G1 --> H4[Entropy]
    G1 --> I1[Feature Vector]
    D2 --> E2[Threshold Neighbourhood]
    E2 --> F2[Compute LBP Code]
    F2 --> G2[Histogram of Codes]
    G2 --> I2[Feature Vector]
    D3 --> E3[Apply Gabor Filter Bank]
    E3 --> F3[Energy Maps per Scale]
    F3 --> G3[Concatenate Energies]
    G3 --> I3[Feature Vector]
    I1 --> J[Classifier SVM or KNN]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Texture Class Label]
```

**Description of the pipeline:** The input image is converted to greyscale and routed through one or more texture-analysis methods. The statistical path (GLCM) quantises the image, builds a co-occurrence matrix, and extracts Haralick descriptors. The structural-statistical path (LBP) computes local binary codes and pools them into a histogram. The spectral path (Gabor) projects the image onto a multi-scale, multi-orientation filter bank. All three produce a feature vector that is fed to a classifier (SVM, K-NN, or a neural network) which outputs the texture class label.

## 4.2 Occluding Contour Detection — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RGB Image] --> B[Depth or Surface Normal Estimation]
    B --> B1[Shape from Shading]
    B --> B2[Stereo Depth Map]
    B --> B3[Structured Light Depth]
    A --> C[Edge Detection Canny]
    B --> D[Compute N dot V]
    D --> E[Scalar Field of N dot V]
    E --> F[Zero Crossing Detection]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Link Contour Pixels]
    G --> H[Occluding Contour Curve]
    H --> I[3D Shape from Contour]
    H --> J[Visual Hull Reconstruction]
    H --> K[Silhouette for Tracking]
```

**Description:** The system takes an RGB image plus a depth/normal estimate (from one of three sources). It computes the scalar field $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{V}$ and finds its zero-crossings. Canny edges provide additional localisation. The contours are linked to produce a closed occluding boundary, which is then used for 3D shape recovery, visual-hull reconstruction, or silhouette-based tracking.

## 4.3 Optical Flow via Regularization — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame at time t] --> B[Image Sequence I x y t]
    A2[Frame at time t plus dt] --> B
    B --> C[Compute Spatial Gradients I x and I y]
    B --> D[Compute Temporal Gradient I t]
    C --> E[OFCE I x u plus I y v plus I t equals 0]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Is OFCE enough?}
    F -->|No, Aperture Problem| G[Add Smoothness Prior]
    G --> H[Horn Schunck Functional]
    H --> I[Variational Minimisation]
    I --> J[Euler Lagrange Equations]
    J --> K[Discretise via Laplacian]
    K --> L[Iterative Gauss Seidel]
    L --> M{Converged?}
    M -->|No| L
    M -->|Yes| N[Output Dense Flow Field u v]
    N --> O[Downstream Tasks]
    O --> O1[Motion Segmentation]
    O --> O2[Object Tracking]
    O --> O3[Video Stabilisation]
    O --> O4[Ego Motion Estimation]
```

**Description:** Two consecutive frames yield spatial gradients $I_x, I_y$ and a temporal gradient $I_t$. The OFCE alone is insufficient due to the aperture problem, so a smoothness prior is added, producing the Horn–Schunck functional. Variational minimisation leads to Euler–Lagrange equations, which are discretised and solved by iterative Gauss–Seidel relaxation. Upon convergence, a dense flow field $(u, v)$ is produced and used for higher-level tasks.

## 4.4 Regularization Framework — General Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurements y] --> B[Forward Model A x plus noise]
    B --> C[Ill Posed Inverse Problem]
    C --> D[Data Fidelity Term]
    C --> E[Regulariser Term]
    D --> F[Total Energy E x]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Lagrange Multiplier lambda]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Minimise E x]
    H --> I{E x is quadratic?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Linear System A transpose A plus lambda L transpose L]
    J --> K[Closed Form Solution]
    I -->|No, robust penalty| L[Non Convex Optimisation]
    L --> M[IRLS or Grad Descent]
    K --> N[Reconstructed Solution x hat]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Applications]
    O --> O1[Image Denoising]
    O --> O2[Optical Flow]
    O --> O3[Surface Reconstruction]
    O --> O4[Compressed Sensing]
```

**Description:** A measurement vector $y$ is related to the unknown $x$ via an ill-posed forward model. Regularization forms a total energy $E(x)$ = data term + regulariser, with a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ balancing them. If the energy is quadratic, a closed-form linear system gives the solution. For robust penalties, iterative reweighted least squares (IRLS) or gradient descent is used. The reconstructed $x$ feeds downstream applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and all labels are plain uppercase or sentence-case text without markdown formatting, in accordance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguard.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define texture. Explain the Grey-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) method for texture analysis with any two Haralick features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Texture is a region-level descriptor that characterises the spatial distribution and arrangement of intensity values in a local neighbourhood of an image. It cannot be defined at a single pixel.
- **GLCM construction (1 mark):** The GLCM is a 2D histogram $P(i, j \mid d, \theta)$ that tabulates how often a pixel of grey level $i$ occurs at a displacement $(d, \theta)$ from a pixel of grey level $j$. The matrix is then normalised to convert counts to probabilities.
- **Two Haralick features (1 mark):**
  - **Contrast** $= \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ — measures local intensity variation; high for sharp textures.
  - **Energy** (Angular Second Moment) $= \sum_{i,j} P(i,j)^2$ — measures uniformity; high for smooth, homogeneous textures.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**What is an occluding contour? How does it differ from a surface contour?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Occluding contour definition (1.5 marks):** An occluding contour is the set of points on a 3D object where the **surface normal is perpendicular to the viewing direction** ($\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$). It marks the silhouette of the object as seen from the camera. It is purely a *viewing-geometry* phenomenon, not an intrinsic surface property.
- **Surface contour (1 mark):** A surface contour is a depth or orientation discontinuity on the surface itself (e.g., the edge of a printed letter, a fold in a cloth). It is *view-independent* and is caused by reflectance or geometry changes on the object.
- **Key difference (0.5 marks):** Occluding contours change as the viewpoint moves; surface contours remain attached to the object. Occluding contours provide 3D shape cues; surface contours provide surface detail cues.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Horn–Schunck method** for computing optical flow. Derive the **Euler–Lagrange equations** from the variational functional. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **regularization parameter $\lambda$** in the Horn–Schunck formulation. What happens when $\lambda \to 0$ and $\lambda \to \infty$? Compare the Horn–Schunck method with the **Lucas–Kanade** method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) — Horn–Schunck method and Euler–Lagrange derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Optical flow constraint (2 marks):**  
Assuming brightness constancy, we have $I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\,dt, y + v\,dt, t + dt)$. A first-order Taylor expansion gives:
$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$
This is a single equation in two unknowns — the **aperture problem**.

**Step 2 — Variational formulation (2 marks):**  
Horn and Schunck propose minimising:
$$E(u, v) = \iint \left[ (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \lambda (u_x^2 + u_y^2 + v_x^2 + v_y^2) \right] dx\, dy$$
The first term is the data fidelity; the second is the smoothness regulariser; $\lambda > 0$ is the trade-off.

**Step 3 — Euler–Lagrange derivation (3 marks):**  
Treating $E$ as a functional of $u$ and $v$, the Euler–Lagrange equations are:
$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial u} - \frac{d}{dx}\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_x} - \frac{d}{dy}\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_y} = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial v} - \frac{d}{dx}\frac{\partial F}{\partial v_x} - \frac{d}{dy}\frac{\partial F}{\partial v_y} = 0$$
Computing the partial derivatives:
- $\frac{\partial F}{\partial u} = 2 I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)$
- $\frac{\partial F}{\partial u_x} = 2\lambda u_x$, and $\frac{d}{dx}(2\lambda u_x) = 2\lambda u_{xx}$

Substituting:
$$2 I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - 2\lambda (u_{xx} + u_{yy}) = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow I_x (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda \nabla^2 u = 0$$
By symmetry:
$$I_y (I_x u + I_y v + I_t) - \lambda \nabla^2 v = 0$$

**Block diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Image sequence → compute $I_x, I_y, I_t$ → form Horn–Schunck functional → solve Euler–Lagrange → iterative Gauss–Seidel → output flow field $(u, v)$.

---

**(b) — Role of $\lambda$ and comparison with Lucas–Kanade (7 marks):**

**Role of $\lambda$ (3.5 marks):**
- $\lambda$ is the **regularization weight** that controls the balance between data fidelity and smoothness.
- **$\lambda \to 0$**: The smoothness term is suppressed. The OFCE alone is solved, which is ill-posed. Numerically, the flow field becomes noisy, and the iterative update reduces to a local least-squares solution at each pixel.
- **$\lambda \to \infty$**: The smoothness term dominates. The flow field becomes a constant (the global average), and all detail is lost. The solution is heavily over-smoothed.
- **Moderate $\lambda$**: Yields a smooth flow field that still respects the data. Typical values are $10^{-2}$ to $10^2$ depending on the image and noise.

**Comparison Horn–Schunck vs. Lucas–Kanade (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Horn–Schunck | Lucas–Kanade |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Global (variational) | Local (least-squares) |
| Output | Dense flow field | Sparse flow (at corners) |
| Smoothness | Global smoothness prior | None (no prior) |
| Computation | Iterative PDE solve | Closed-form over a window |
| Robustness | Over-smooths motion boundaries | Preserves discontinuities |
| Use case | Dense scene flow | Feature tracking, SLAM |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a)** Explain **Local Binary Pattern (LBP)** for texture analysis. Derive the LBP code for a $3 \times 3$ neighbourhood with an example. Discuss the concept of **uniform LBP**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **regularization theory** in the context of computer vision. Explain the **Tikhonov regularization** framework and the role of the **Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$**. Describe **discontinuity-preserving regularization** with one example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) — LBP and uniform LBP (7 marks):**

**LBP algorithm (3 marks):**  
Given a centre pixel $i_c$ and $P$ neighbours on a circle of radius $R$, the LBP code is:
$$\text{LBP}_{P,R} = \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(i_p - i_c) \cdot 2^p$$
where $s(z) = 1$ if $z \ge 0$, else $0$.

**Example computation (2 marks):**  
Consider a $3 \times 3$ neighbourhood with centre $i_c = 50$ and neighbours (clockwise from top-left): `45, 60, 80, 30, 55, 70, 35, 40`.
- Differences: `-5, +10, +30, -20, +5, +20, -15, -10`
- Sign codes: `0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0` (weight $2^0$ to $2^7$).
- LBP = $0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 4 + 0\cdot 8 + 1\cdot 16 + 1\cdot 32 + 0\cdot 64 + 0\cdot 128 = 54$.

**Uniform LBP (2 marks):**  
A uniform LBP is one in which the binary code has **at most two bitwise transitions** (0→1 or 1→0) when read cyclically. For example, `00001111` is uniform (one transition), `01010101` is not (many transitions). Uniform LBPs cover ~90% of all patterns in natural textures. They provide a compact, rotation-invariant descriptor.

---

**(b) — Regularization theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Regularization theory is a mathematical framework for solving **ill-posed inverse problems** by adding a priori constraints (regularizers) to the data fidelity term, producing a unique, stable solution.

**Tikhonov framework (3 marks):**  
For a linear problem $y = Ax + n$, Tikhonov regularization solves:
$$\hat{x} = \arg\min_x \left[ \|Ax - y\|^2 + \lambda \|Lx\|^2 \right]$$
where $L$ is typically the identity (penalises magnitude) or a derivative operator (penalises roughness), and $\lambda > 0$ is the Lagrange multiplier. The closed-form solution is:
$$\hat{x} = (A^\top A + \lambda L^\top L)^{-1} A^\top y$$
This is a *biased estimator* — it sacrifices a small amount of variance reduction in exchange for a large reduction in variance (a *bias–variance trade-off*).

**Role of $\lambda$ (1.5 marks):**
- Small $\lambda$ → low bias, high variance, possibly unstable.
- Large $\lambda$ → high bias, low variance, over-smoothed.
- $\lambda$ is chosen by the **L-curve method**, **cross-validation**, or **Morozov's discrepancy principle**.

**Discontinuity-preserving regularisation (1.5 marks):**
The quadratic penalty $\|Lx\|^2$ over-smooths sharp transitions. Replace it with a robust function $\psi(\cdot)$:
$$\hat{x} = \arg\min_x \left[ \|Ax - y\|^2 + \lambda \sum_i \psi\big((Lx)_i\big) \right]$$
Example: **Total Variation** uses $\psi(s) = |s|$, which preserves sharp edges by penalising the *magnitude* of gradients rather than their *square*. This is widely used in image denoising (Rudin–Osher–Fatemi model).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. **Confusing optical flow with motion field.** Many students write "optical flow is the actual motion" — it is the *apparent* motion of brightness, which equals the motion field only under brightness constancy. (Penalty: -1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Euler–Lagrange.** When expanding $(I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2$, the derivative w.r.t. $u$ gives $2 I_x (\cdot)$, not $I_x (\cdot)$. Forgetting the 2 leads to a wrong sign. (Penalty: -0.5 to -1 mark.)
> 3. **Mixing LBP code direction.** LBP bit-ordering (clockwise vs. anti-clockwise) is not standardised. State your convention explicitly, or you will be marked inconsistent. (Penalty: -0.5 mark.)
> 4. **GLCM normalisation.** A common error is using raw counts instead of normalised probabilities. The features are *defined* on the normalised matrix. (Penalty: -1 mark.)
> 5. **Confusing Tikhonov with Ridge regression.** Tikhonov regularisation *is* ridge regression in linear regression, but in vision it is applied to inverse problems like deblurring, not just regression. Make the context clear. (Penalty: -1 mark.)
> 6. **Writing $\nabla^2 u$ without explanation.** Examiners want $\nabla^2 u = u_{xx} + u_{yy} = \bar{u} - u$ (discrete Laplacian). Students who omit the definition lose easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Texture, Occluding Contours, Motion, Regularization):**

- [ ] **Texture** is a *region-level* property, not a point property. Two paradigms: **statistical** (GLCM, LBP) and **spectral** (Gabor).
- [ ] **GLCM** is built at a chosen offset $(d, \theta)$. The matrix is normalised before feature extraction. Four must-know features: **Energy, Contrast, Homogeneity, Entropy**.
- [ ] **LBP** uses a signum function on the centre–neighbour differences; bit-weighting is $2^p$. **Uniform LBP** restricts to ≤ 2 bit transitions.
- [ ] **Gabor filters** are Gaussian-modulated sinusoids. A *filter bank* at multiple scales and orientations captures texture energy.
- [ ] **Occluding contours** satisfy $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$ — the dot product of surface normal and viewing direction. They form the silhouette and change with viewpoint.
- [ ] **Surface contours** are view-independent (intrinsic edges), unlike occluding contours.
- [ ] **Optical flow** = apparent motion of brightness; **Motion field** = true 2D projection of 3D motion. They are equal under brightness constancy.
- [ ] **OFCE**: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ — one equation, two unknowns (aperture problem).
- [ ] **Horn–Schunck functional**: $E = \iint [(I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \lambda (\|\nabla u\|^2 + \|\nabla v\|^2)] \, dx\, dy$.
- [ ] **Euler–Lagrange equations** of Horn–Schunck: $I_x(\cdot) - \lambda \nabla^2 u = 0$ and $I_y(\cdot) - \lambda \nabla^2 v = 0$.
- [ ] **Iterative Gauss–Seidel update** (discrete): $u^{n+1} = \bar{u}^n - \frac{I_x (I_x \bar{u}^n + I_y \bar{v}^n + I_t)}{\lambda + I_x^2 + I_y^2}$.
- [ ] **$\lambda$** is the regularisation weight. $\lambda \to 0$ → noisy; $\lambda \to \infty$ → over-smoothed.
- [ ] **Lucas–Kanade** is a *local* method using least-squares in a small window; produces *sparse* flow.
- [ ] **Horn–Schunck** is a *global* variational method; produces *dense* smooth flow.
- [ ] **Regularization theory** = framework to solve ill-posed inverse problems by adding a priori constraints.
- [ ] **Tikhonov** regularisation: $\min_x \|Ax - y\|^2 + \lambda \|Lx\|^2$. Closed-form via normal equations.
- [ ] **Discontinuity-preserving** regularisation uses a robust $\psi(\cdot)$ — examples: $L_1$ (Total Variation), Huber, Lorentzian.
- [ ] **Total Variation (TV)**: $\int \|\nabla u\| \, dx\, dy$ — preserves edges; basis of the Rudin–Osher–Fatemi denoising model.
- [ ] **L-curve method** is the standard way to choose $\lambda$ — plot $\|Ax - y\|$ vs. $\|Lx\|$ on log–log axes and pick the corner.
- [ ] **Real-world applications** to remember: defect detection (texture), visual hull (occluding contour), video stabilisation (optical flow), image denoising (regularization).
- [ ] **Key paper citations** to memorise: Haralick 1973 (GLCM), Ojala 1996 (LBP), Horn & Schunck 1981 (optical flow), Tikhonov 1963 (regularization), Rudin–Osher–Fatemi 1992 (TV denoising).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Flow - Brightness Constancy Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT833 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Flow - Brightness Constancy Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optical Flow — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Optical Flow** is defined as the distribution of apparent velocities of brightness patterns (intensity patterns) in an image sequence. In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terms, it is the **2D vector field describing the motion of pixel intensities between consecutive frames** of a video sequence, caused by the relative motion between the camera and the observed scene.

Mathematically, optical flow assigns a 2D velocity vector $\mathbf{v} = (u, v)$ to every pixel $(x, y)$ in the image, where:
- $u = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ — the horizontal component of apparent motion
- $v = \dfrac{dy}{dt}$ — the vertical component of apparent motion

The complete set of such vectors across the image forms the **optical flow field** $\{(u(x,y), v(x,y))\}_{x,y \in \text{Image}}$.

### 1.2 The Brightness Constancy Assumption (BCA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brightness Constancy Assumption:** The intensity (brightness) of a particular point on a moving object remains *unchanged* between consecutive frames, even though its spatial position changes.

Formally, if a pixel is located at position $(x, y)$ at time $t$ with intensity $I(x, y, t)$, then after moving by a small displacement $(dx, dy)$ over a small time interval $dt$ to position $(x+dx, y+dy)$ at time $(t+dt)$, its intensity remains the same:

$$I(x + dx,\ y + dy,\ t + dt) = I(x,\ y,\ t)$$

This assumption is the *cornerstone* of gradient-based optical flow estimation and was first introduced by **Horn and Schunck (1981)**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Moving Coin on a Table

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are looking down at a coin rolling across a wooden table under steady indoor lighting. The coin's surface (gray-silver) appears with the **same brightness** at every frame, but its **pixel location** in your eye/camera shifts. Your visual system effortlessly tracks this motion by matching the *same brightness pattern* to its new location. The brain essentially assumes "the brightness of this object doesn't suddenly change" — that is the **Brightness Constancy Assumption** in action.

If, however, a sudden light flicker occurs or the coin enters a shadow, the assumption **breaks down**, and our motion perception (and any computer vision algorithm relying on it) becomes unreliable. This explains why algorithms fail under changing illumination.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Computer Vision

Optical flow is critical for:

| Application Domain | Use of Optical Flow |
|---|---|
| **Video Surveillance** | Tracking moving objects (people, vehicles) |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Detecting motion of surrounding vehicles and pedestrians |
| **Action Recognition** | Recognizing human activities from motion patterns |
| **3D Reconstruction** | Estimating scene depth from motion (Structure-from-Motion) |
| **Video Compression** | MPEG/H.264 use motion vectors to compress inter-frames |
| **Robotics / Drone Navigation** | Visual Odometry and ego-motion estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU parlance, the **standard reference value** for typical frame rates used in optical flow literature is **$f = 30\ \text{fps}$** (frames per second) and inter-frame displacement is typically $\mathbf{1}$ to $\mathbf{5}$ pixels in modern dense flow benchmarks (KITTI, Sintel, FlyingChairs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Building Blocks

To derive and apply the Brightness Constancy Equation, we need four foundational quantities:

- **Spatial Gradient in x-direction:** $I_x = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x}$ — measures how intensity changes horizontally
- **Spatial Gradient in y-direction:** $I_y = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y}$ — measures how intensity changes vertically
- **Temporal Gradient:** $I_t = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial t}$ — measures how intensity changes over time
- **Optical Flow Vector:** $\mathbf{v} = (u, v)$ — the unknown we wish to recover

### 2.2 The Brightness Constancy Equation (BCE) — Derivation Logic

The core idea unfolds in three logical steps:

1. **Assume brightness is preserved** as a point moves from frame to frame.
2. **Linearize** the resulting nonlinear constraint using a first-order **Taylor Series Expansion** (valid only for small motions).
3. **Rearrange** to obtain a single linear equation in the two unknown velocity components.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation you must memorize** for solving optical flow problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Symbol Meaning | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brightness Constancy Assumption | $I(x+dx,\,y+dy,\,t+dt) = I(x,y,t)$ | Intensity equality | Strictly exact only under constant illumination |
| 2 | First-order Taylor Expansion | $I(x+\Delta,y+\Delta,t+\Delta) \approx I + I_x\,dx + I_y\,dy + I_t\,dt$ | Linearization | Valid only for **small** $(dx,dy,dt)$ |
| 3 | **Brightness Constancy Equation (BCE)** | $I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0$ | The fundamental flow constraint | One equation, **two unknowns** $(u,v)$ |
| 4 | Vector Form of BCE | $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0$ | $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ | Compact vector notation |
| 5 | Component Form | $u \cdot \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x} + v \cdot \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial t}$ | Explicit component form | Alternative rearrangement |
| 6 | Normal Flow Component | $v_n = \dfrac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$ | Flow along gradient direction | Minimum-norm solution of BCE |
| 7 | Gradient Magnitude | $\Vert \nabla I \Vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | Strength of image edge | Detector for *textured* regions |
| 8 | Optical Flow Constraint Line | $u \cdot I_x + v \cdot I_y = -I_t$ | Linear constraint line in $(u,v)$ space | The solution lies on this line |
| 9 | Lucas-Kanade Equation | $A^T A\, \mathbf{v} = A^T \mathbf{b}$ with $A = [I_x\ \ I_y]$, $\mathbf{b} = -I_t$ | System of $n$ equations | Solved by **least-squares** |
| 10 | Lucas-Kanade Solution | $\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | Closed-form flow vector | Requires $A^T A$ to be **invertible** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Optical flow is used to track the beating of the heart in echocardiograms and to analyze blood cell movement in microscopy.
- **Sports Analytics:** Hawk-Eye systems in tennis and cricket use optical flow to track ball trajectories in real time.
- **Astronomy:** Tracking the apparent motion of celestial objects and solar plasma flows on the Sun's surface.
- **Industry:** Automated quality control on conveyor belts — detecting defective parts by their anomalous motion patterns.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** The BCE is reliable when image gradients are strong (edge-rich regions) and breaks down in **flat, textureless** regions. This directly motivates the **Aperture Problem** and motivates the need for multi-point, weighted estimation schemes like **Lucas-Kanade** and **Horn-Schunck**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Brightness Constancy Equation

We now derive the BCE from the brightness constancy assumption in complete, step-by-step detail. **Every algebraic manipulation is shown explicitly** — no step is skipped.

---

#### **Step 1: State the Brightness Constancy Assumption**

Let $I(x, y, t)$ denote the image intensity at spatial location $(x, y)$ at time $t$. A particular physical point in the scene is observed at this pixel location at time $t$. After a small time interval $dt$, this same point moves to a new location $(x + dx, y + dy)$ on the image plane. The brightness constancy assumption states that the intensity value of this point remains unchanged:

$$I(x + dx,\ y + dy,\ t + dt) = I(x,\ y,\ t)$$

**Why:** This equation encodes the physics that illumination is constant and the surface reflectance of the object point does not change as it moves.

---

#### **Step 2: Apply a First-Order Taylor Series Expansion**

We Taylor-expand the left-hand side about the point $(x, y, t)$. The first-order (linear) Taylor expansion of a multivariate function $f(x, y, t)$ about point $(x_0, y_0, t_0)$ is:

$$f(x_0 + \Delta x,\, y_0 + \Delta y,\, t_0 + \Delta t) \approx f(x_0, y_0, t_0) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{0} \Delta x + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\bigg|_{0} \Delta y + \frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\bigg|_{0} \Delta t$$

Applying this with $f = I$, $(x_0, y_0, t_0) = (x, y, t)$, and $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta t) = (dx, dy, dt)$:

$$I(x + dx,\, y + dy,\, t + dt) \approx I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\, dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\, dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\, dt$$

**Why:** We truncate the Taylor series at first order to obtain a *linear* approximation. This is mathematically valid only when $(dx, dy, dt)$ are small, which is the case when the inter-frame motion is small (typically 1-2 pixels per frame).

---

#### **Step 3: Substitute the Taylor Expansion into the Brightness Constancy Equation**

Replacing the left-hand side of the BCA with the Taylor-expanded form:

$$I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\, dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\, dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\, dt \approx I(x, y, t)$$

---

#### **Step 4: Simplify by Cancelling $I(x, y, t)$ on Both Sides**

Subtract $I(x, y, t)$ from both sides of the equation:

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\, dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\, dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\, dt = 0$$

Using the notation $I_x = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x}$, $I_y = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y}$, $I_t = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial t}$:

$$I_x \cdot dx + I_y \cdot dy + I_t \cdot dt = 0$$

---

#### **Step 5: Divide the Entire Equation by $dt$**

Dividing each term by $dt$ (a small, non-zero time interval):

$$I_x \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + I_y \cdot \frac{dy}{dt} + I_t \cdot \frac{dt}{dt} = 0$$

Recognizing the definitions of velocity components $u = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $v = \dfrac{dy}{dt}$, and $\dfrac{dt}{dt} = 1$:

$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0$$

---

#### **Step 6: Final Form — The Brightness Constancy Equation (BCE)**

$$\boxed{I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0}$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)**, also known as the **Brightness Constancy Equation (BCE)**. In compact vector form:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \nabla I = (I_x, I_y),\ \mathbf{v} = (u, v)$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Normal Flow Component

Although the BCE has two unknowns $(u, v)$, we can compute the component of flow *along* the image gradient direction. The dot product form is:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} = -I_t$$

Projecting $\mathbf{v}$ onto the unit gradient direction $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \dfrac{\nabla I}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert}$:

$$v_n = \frac{\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert} = \frac{-I_t}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert} = \frac{-I_t}{\sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}}$$

This $v_n$ is called the **normal flow** — it is the component of motion perpendicular to the local image edge.

### 3.3 The Aperture Problem — Why BCE Alone Is Insufficient

> [!WARNING]
> The BCE provides **one linear equation** but contains **two unknowns** $(u, v)$. Geometrically, in the $(u,v)$ velocity space, the equation defines a **straight line** of possible solutions — not a single point. This is the famous **Aperture Problem**.

An observer looking through a small aperture (local window) sees only a moving edge; the motion of the edge *along* itself is invisible. Only the **perpendicular** component of motion can be measured. This is why the **normal flow** $v_n$ is all that the BCE can determine locally.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solutions to the Aperture Problem

| Method | Strategy | Equations Used | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lucas-Kanade (1981)** | Assume flow is **constant** in a local $N \times N$ patch | Stack $N^2$ BCEs, solve via least-squares | Fast, closed-form, robust to noise | Fails for large motions |
| **Horn-Schunck (1981)** | Add a **smoothness regularizer** $\Vert \nabla u \Vert^2 + \Vert \nabla v \Vert^2$ | BCE + global smoothness penalty | Produces dense flow fields | Iterative, computationally heavier |
| **Pyramid / Coarse-to-Fine** | Estimate flow at coarse scale, refine at finer scales | Recursive BCE on image pyramid | Handles **large motions** | More complex implementation |
| **Farneback (2003)** | Polynomial expansion of local signal | Dense quadratic fitting | Very dense flow fields | Sensitive to noise |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Illustrative)

**Given:** $I_x = 4$, $I_y = 3$, $I_t = -10$. **Find:** the normal flow $v_n$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the gradient magnitude.

$$\Vert \nabla I \Vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

Step 2: Apply the normal flow formula.

$$v_n = \frac{-I_t}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert} = \frac{-(-10)}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2\ \text{pixels per frame}$$

**Interpretation:** The local image content is moving with a component of velocity equal to $2$ pixels/frame along the gradient direction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Optical Flow Estimation Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the complete functional pipeline for estimating optical flow using the Brightness Constancy Equation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT FRAME AT TIME T] --> C[IMAGE GRADIENT COMPUTATION]
    B[INPUT FRAME AT TIME T PLUS 1] --> C
    C --> D[IX SPATIAL GRADIENT X]
    C --> E[IY SPATIAL GRADIENT Y]
    C --> F[IT TEMPORAL GRADIENT]
    D --> G[BRIGHTNESS CONSTANCY EQUATION]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[APERTURE PROBLEM ONE EQUATION TWO UNKNOWNS]
    H --> I[ADDITIONAL CONSTRAINT NEEDED]
    I --> J[LUCAS KANADE LOCAL CONSTANCY]
    I --> K[HORN SCHUNCK GLOBAL SMOOTHNESS]
    I --> L[PYRAMID COARSE TO FINE]
    J --> M[OPTICAL FLOW FIELD U V]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[OUTPUT MOTION VECTORS]
```

### 4.2 Derivation Flow Diagram — From Assumption to Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[BRIGHTNESS CONSTANCY ASSUMPTION] --> S2[EQUATION IX PLUS DX Y PLUS DY T PLUS DT EQUALS IX Y T]
    S2 --> S3[FIRST ORDER TAYLOR SERIES EXPANSION]
    S3 --> S4[LINEAR APPROXIMATION]
    S4 --> S5[CANCEL COMMON TERM IXYT]
    S5 --> S6[DIVIDE BOTH SIDES BY DT]
    S6 --> S7[DEFINE U EQUALS DX BY DT AND V EQUALS DY BY DT]
    S7 --> S8[FINAL BRIGHTNESS CONSTANCY EQUATION IX U PLUS IY V PLUS IT EQUALS ZERO]
    S8 --> S9[APERTURE PROBLEM DETECTED]
    S9 --> S10[NEED REGULARIZATION OR LOCAL ASSUMPTION]
```

### 4.3 Geometric Interpretation — The Constraint Line in Velocity Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VELOCITY_SPACE_UV[VELOCITY SPACE U V]
        P1[ORIGIN 0 0] --> P2[CONSTRAINT LINE IX U PLUS IY V PLUS IT EQUALS ZERO]
    end
    VN[OBSERVED NORMAL FLOW Vn] -.PERPENDICULAR PROJECTION.-> P2
    P2 --> SOL[INFINITE FAMILY OF POSSIBLE FLOWS ALONG THIS LINE]
    SOL --> AP[APERTURE PROBLEM]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Lucas-Kanade Local Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[LOCAL N BY N PATCH] --> Q[STACK N SQUARED BRIGHTNESS CONSTANCY EQUATIONS]
    Q --> R[FORM MATRIX A WITH IX IY COLUMNS]
    Q --> S[FORM VECTOR B WITH NEGATIVE IT VALUES]
    R --> T[COMPUTE A TRANSPOSE A]
    S --> U[COMPUTE A TRANSPOSE B]
    T --> V[SOLVE V EQUALS A TRANSPOSE A INVERSE A TRANSPOSE B]
    U --> V
    V --> W[OUTPUT FLOW VECTOR U V]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define optical flow and state the brightness constancy assumption with its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** *Optical flow* is the 2D velocity field that describes the apparent motion of brightness patterns between two consecutive image frames. Each pixel $(x, y)$ is assigned a velocity vector $\mathbf{v} = (u, v)$ where $u = dx/dt$ and $v = dy/dt$.

- **(1 mark)** *Brightness Constancy Assumption:* The intensity of a point on a moving object remains the same between consecutive frames.

- **(1 mark)** *Mathematical form:* $I(x + dx,\ y + dy,\ t + dt) = I(x,\ y,\ t)$, where $I$ is image intensity and $(dx, dy, dt)$ is the small displacement and time interval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the aperture problem in optical flow estimation? How is it related to the brightness constancy equation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The *aperture problem* refers to the fact that the brightness constancy equation provides only **one linear equation** in **two unknown** flow components $(u, v)$ — leading to an infinite family of possible solutions at any local neighborhood.

- **(1 mark)** Geometrically, the BCE defines a *constraint line* in the $(u, v)$ velocity space; the actual flow vector lies on this line but its precise location along the line is undetermined from local measurements alone.

- **(1 mark)** It arises because a small local aperture can only detect motion *perpendicular* to image edges (the *normal flow*), not motion along the edges themselves. Solutions such as the Lucas-Kanade or Horn-Schunck methods add additional constraints (local constancy or global smoothness) to resolve this ambiguity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Derive the Brightness Constancy Equation from the brightness constancy assumption using a first-order Taylor series expansion. Clearly define all symbols used. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the aperture problem in detail. How does the Lucas-Kanade method resolve it? Show the matrix form of the Lucas-Kanade equations. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the brightness constancy assumption.

$$I(x + dx,\, y + dy,\, t + dt) = I(x, y, t)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the first-order Taylor expansion of $I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt)$ about $(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt) \approx I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} dt$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute into the BCA equation:

$$I(x, y, t) + I_x\, dx + I_y\, dy + I_t\, dt = I(x, y, t)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Cancel $I(x, y, t)$ from both sides:

$$I_x\, dx + I_y\, dy + I_t\, dt = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Divide throughout by $dt$:

$$I_x \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + I_y \cdot \frac{dy}{dt} + I_t = 0$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$ to obtain the BCE:

$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Provide the vector form and symbol definitions:

$$\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0, \quad \text{where } \nabla I = (I_x, I_y), \mathbf{v} = (u, v)$$

**Symbol Definitions:**
- $I_x = \partial I / \partial x$ — spatial intensity gradient in x-direction
- $I_y = \partial I / \partial y$ — spatial intensity gradient in y-direction
- $I_t = \partial I / \partial t$ — temporal intensity gradient
- $u, v$ — horizontal and vertical components of optical flow

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Explain the aperture problem.

The aperture problem arises because the BCE contains two unknowns $(u, v)$ but only one equation. In the $(u, v)$ velocity space, the equation $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ defines a straight line. Any vector on this line is a mathematically valid solution. Physically, when a small aperture observes a moving edge, it can only detect the motion *perpendicular* to the edge (the **normal flow** $v_n = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$). The motion *parallel* to the edge is invisible.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The Lucas-Kanade assumption.

Lucas and Kanade (1981) resolve this by assuming that the flow vector is **approximately constant** in a small local neighborhood (typically an $N \times N$ patch, e.g., $5 \times 5$). This yields $N^2$ BCEs from the patch, all sharing the same $(u, v)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Matrix formulation.

For each pixel $i$ in the patch, the BCE is: $I_x^{(i)} u + I_y^{(i)} v = -I_t^{(i)}$.

Stacking these into matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_x^{(1)} & I_y^{(1)} \\ I_x^{(2)} & I_y^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_x^{(N^2)} & I_y^{(N^2)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -I_t^{(1)} \\ -I_t^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ -I_t^{(N^2)} \end{bmatrix}$$

In compact form: $A\, \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{b}$ where $A = [I_x\ \ I_y]$ and $\mathbf{b} = -I_t$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Least-squares closed-form solution.

$$\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

This requires $A^T A$ to be invertible, which is satisfied in image regions with **strong intensity gradients** in *both* x and y directions (i.e., corners / textured regions), not along uniform edges.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a) State the brightness constancy assumption. With suitable diagrams, derive the optical flow constraint equation and discuss its physical interpretation. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the limitations of the brightness constancy equation. Explain how the Horn-Schunck method extends the BCE with a global smoothness constraint. Write the Horn-Schunck energy functional. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the BCA.

The intensity of a given scene point does not change as it moves across frames: $I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt) = I(x, y, t)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Taylor expansion.

$$I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt) \approx I(x, y, t) + I_x\, dx + I_y\, dy + I_t\, dt$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Derive the OFCE.

After simplification and division by $dt$:

$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} = -I_t$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Physical interpretation.

- $I_x$, $I_y$ are the **spatial gradients** of the image — they describe how the image brightness changes across space.
- $I_t$ is the **temporal gradient** — how brightness changes from one frame to the next.
- The equation states: the dot product of the **local image gradient** with the **flow vector** equals the **negative temporal brightness change**.
- The flow component along the gradient direction is uniquely determined: it is the **normal flow**.
- The component of flow *tangent* to image edges is invisible from the local BCE — this is the aperture problem.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Limitations of the BCE.

- **Aperture problem** — one equation, two unknowns.
- **Brightness constancy violation** — changes in illumination, shadows, or specular reflections invalidate the BCA.
- **Large motions** — the first-order Taylor expansion is valid only for small displacements (typically 1-2 pixels per frame).
- **Occlusions** — newly appearing or disappearing pixels cannot be modeled.
- **Textureless regions** — flat areas have $\nabla I \approx 0$, making $I_x$ and $I_y$ numerically unstable.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Horn-Schunck extension.

Horn and Schunck (1981) resolve the under-determined BCE by imposing a **global smoothness constraint** on the flow field, requiring $(u, v)$ to vary smoothly across the entire image. The solution minimizes the combined energy functional:

$$E(u, v) = \underbrace{\iint \left( I_x u + I_y v + I_t \right)^2 dx\, dy}_{\text{Data term (BCE squared)}} + \underbrace{\alpha \iint \left( \Vert \nabla u \Vert^2 + \Vert \nabla v \Vert^2 \right) dx\, dy}_{\text{Smoothness term}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Explanation of terms.

- The **first term** penalizes violation of the BCE (data fidelity).
- The **second term** penalizes rapid spatial variations in $u$ and $v$ (smoothness).
- $\alpha > 0$ is a regularization weight balancing the two terms; larger $\alpha$ enforces smoother flow fields.
- The result is a pair of Euler-Lagrange equations that are solved iteratively (often via successive over-relaxation) to yield a **dense flow field**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumption explicitly** before derivation. Always *begin* with the BCA equation. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Skipping the Taylor expansion step** or writing it as a single line. Examiners award marks for *each* transitional step. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Not defining symbols** like $u$, $v$, $I_x$, $I_y$, $I_t$ — these must always be defined. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing the BCE with the Lucas-Kanade equation** — BCE is a single equation; Lucas-Kanade is a *system* of equations solved by least-squares. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 5. **Failing to mention the aperture problem explicitly** when discussing BCE limitations. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not mentioning the small-motion assumption** behind the first-order Taylor linearization. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Optical flow** = apparent 2D motion of brightness patterns in image sequences.
- ✅ **Brightness Constancy Assumption (BCA):** $I(x+dx,\,y+dy,\,t+dt) = I(x,y,t)$.
- ✅ **Brightness Constancy Equation (BCE / OFCE):** $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$.
- ✅ **Vector form:** $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0$ where $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$.
- ✅ **Symbols:** $u = dx/dt$, $v = dy/dt$, $I_x = \partial I/\partial x$, $I_y = \partial I/\partial y$, $I_t = \partial I/\partial t$.
- ✅ **Derivation uses first-order Taylor series expansion** (valid only for *small* motions).
- ✅ **Aperture problem:** BCE is *one* equation with *two* unknowns — solution lies on a constraint line in $(u,v)$ space.
- ✅ **Normal flow:** $v_n = -I_t / \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ — flow along the gradient direction (the only locally computable component).
- ✅ **Lucas-Kanade method:** assumes flow is constant in a local patch; uses least-squares: $\mathbf{v} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$.
- ✅ **Horn-Schunck method:** adds global smoothness regularizer; produces dense flow field.
- ✅ **BCE failures:** large motions, changing illumination, occlusions, textureless regions.
- ✅ **Reference works:** **Horn & Schunck (1981)** and **Lucas & Kanade (1981)** — the two foundational papers of optical flow.
- ✅ **Practical frame rate used in benchmarks:** typically **$f = 30\ \text{fps}$**; standard datasets are Sintel, KITTI, and FlyingChairs.
- ✅ **Key insight for exams:** The BCE is a *constraint*, not a *solution*; additional assumptions are always needed to recover $(u, v)$ uniquely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aperture Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT833 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aperture Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Aperture Problem (Formally: The Correspondence Problem of Local Motion Estimation)**
> The Aperture Problem is the inherent ambiguity in estimating the true 2D image motion (optical flow) of a visual feature when measurements are restricted to a *local neighborhood* or *small window* (aperture) around that feature. Because a local detector (e.g., a receptive field, correlation kernel, or gradient-based operator) can only observe the component of motion **perpendicular to the local intensity gradient** (the *normal flow*), the *tangential component* of motion along the edge/contour remains unobservable. Consequently, infinitely many 2D velocity vectors produce identical local image measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Keyhole Illusion"

Imagine you are standing inside a locked room and can only see the outside world through a **small keyhole** in the door.

* A car drives past on the road. Through the keyhole, you only ever see a small patch of the car's surface at any instant.
* From that tiny patch, you can *correctly infer* the component of motion that is **perpendicular to the visible edge** of the car (e.g., the velocity at which the edge is moving across the keyhole).
* However, the **component of motion parallel to the edge** (sliding along the length of the car) is invisible — the edge looks the same whether the car is rolling or sliding sideways.
* This is *exactly* what happens to a local computer vision operator (such as a 7×7 Lucas–Kanade window) when it views a straight contour. Only the **normal flow** is recoverable locally; the **tangential flow** is unobservable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The Aperture Problem is *not* a failure of the algorithm — it is a **fundamental information-theoretic limitation**. No local method can resolve it; only *global* constraints (smoothness priors, second-derivative structure, multiple apertures, or higher-order statistics) can disambiguate the true motion.

### 1.3 Core Entities in the Aperture Problem

| Entity | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Image intensity | $I(x, y, t)$ | Brightness at pixel $(x,y)$ at time $t$ |
| True 2D velocity | $\mathbf{v} = (u, v)$ | The unknown real motion in the image plane |
| Spatial gradient | $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ | Local image derivative |
| Normal flow | $v_n$ | The *observable* component of $\mathbf{v}$ along $\nabla I$ |
| Tangential flow | $v_t$ | The *unobservable* component perpendicular to $\nabla I$ |
| Aperture | $\Omega$ | The local measurement window (e.g., $5\times 5$ or $7\times 7$) |
| **Brightness Constancy Constant** | — | Assumes $I(x, y, t) = I(x+u, y+v, t+1)$ |

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

* **Normalization Constant for Unit Vector:** $1$ (dimensionless) — used in projection $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \nabla I / \Vert \nabla I \Vert$.
* **Minimum Aperture Radius (rule of thumb):** **5×5 pixels** to obtain a stable gradient estimate; smaller windows amplify the aperture ambiguity.
* **Optical Flow Unit:** *pixels/frame* (or *pixels/second* in continuous-time formulations).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Assumption — Brightness Constancy

The entire edifice of gradient-based optical flow rests on a single assumption:

> The intensity of a moving surface point remains constant between consecutive frames.

Mathematically:

$$I(x, y, t) \;=\; I(x + u\,\Delta t,\; y + v\,\Delta t,\; t + \Delta t)$$

This is the **Brightness Constancy Constraint Equation (BCCE)** — *the* foundational equation in KTU Module 3.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Aperture Constraint (Logical Steps)

1. **Expand the right-hand side** of BCCE using a first-order Taylor series about $(x, y, t)$.
2. **Drop higher-order terms** (valid when motion is small — usually $< 1$ pixel/frame).
3. **Rearrange** to obtain the *Optical Flow Constraint Equation* (OFCE).
4. **Recognize the ambiguity:** the OFCE is a *single scalar equation* in *two unknowns* $(u, v)$ — therefore under-determined.
5. **Project the unknown velocity** onto the gradient direction to isolate the *only* component that the aperture can observe.
6. **Define normal flow** as the projection magnitude — the tangible, measurable quantity.
7. **Declare the tangential component unobservable locally** — this *is* the Aperture Problem.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)** | Continuous image |
| 2 | $\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t = 0$ | Vector form of OFCE | Continuous image |
| 3 | $v_n = -\dfrac{I_t}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert}$ | **Normal Flow** (observable scalar) | Pixel/frame |
| 4 | $v_n = \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ | Geometric definition of normal flow | Pixel/frame |
| 5 | $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \dfrac{\nabla I}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert}$ | Unit normal to local isophote | Unitless |
| 6 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{I_y}{I_x}\right)$ | Orientation of the local edge | Radians |
| 7 | $v_t = \mathbf{v} - v_n \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ | **Tangential Flow** (unobservable locally) | Pixel/frame |
| 8 | $\mathbf{v} = v_n \hat{\mathbf{n}} + v_t \hat{\mathbf{t}}$ | Decomposition into normal + tangential | Pixel/frame |
| 9 | $\min_{\mathbf{v}} \sum_{(x,y) \in \Omega} \left( \nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} + I_t \right)^2$ | **Lucas–Kanade objective** (solves ambiguity over a window) | Per-window |
| 10 | $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{b}$ | Lucas–Kanade normal equations | Matrix form |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TRICK:** Formula 3 contains $\Vert \nabla I \Vert$ in the denominator. When the gradient magnitude is **small** (e.g., inside a flat, textureless region), $v_n$ becomes *numerically unstable* and *blows up*. This is the **"blank wall problem"** — a sibling of the aperture problem. Always mention the *validity condition* $\Vert \nabla I \Vert > \tau$ (a small threshold).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Autonomous vehicles (e.g., KTU alumni at Cruise, Waymo):** Aperture-aware optical flow is critical for collision-time estimation when a pedestrian's edge enters the camera's small receptive field.
* **Robotic surgery (Da Vinci systems):** Endoscopic cameras have tiny apertures — aperture-robust motion estimation prevents tissue-tracking drift.
* **Video compression (HEVC, VVC):** Motion compensation vectors are *under-determined* locally; encoders exploit this by using small blocks (TU/CU partitioning) to localize the ambiguity.
* **Neuromorphic vision (event cameras, DVS):** The aperture problem is *intensified* because each event only signals *change*, not full intensity — extra global regularization is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Brightness Constancy to Normal Flow

We start from the Brightness Constancy hypothesis:

$$I(x, y, t) \;=\; I(x + u\,\Delta t,\; y + v\,\Delta t,\; t + \Delta t)$$

**Step 1 — Taylor Series Expansion of the RHS**

Expand the right-hand side as a multivariate first-order Taylor series around the point $(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x + u\,\Delta t,\; y + v\,\Delta t,\; t + \Delta t) \;\approx\; I(x, y, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}(u\,\Delta t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}(v\,\Delta t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}(\Delta t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Standard Notation**

Let $I_x = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x}$, $I_y = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y}$, $I_t = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial t}$. Then:

$$I(x, y, t) + I_x\, u\,\Delta t + I_y\, v\,\Delta t + I_t\, \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back into BCCE**

$$I(x, y, t) \;=\; I(x, y, t) + I_x\, u\,\Delta t + I_y\, v\,\Delta t + I_t\, \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the Constant $I(x,y,t)$ and Divide by $\Delta t$**

$$0 \;=\; I_x\, u + I_y\, v + I_t$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)**. It is a *single scalar* equation in *two* unknown scalars $(u, v)$ — therefore mathematically *under-determined*.

**Step 5 — Reorganize as a Vector Inner Product**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_x\, u + I_y\, v + I_t &= 0 \\[4pt]
\nabla I \cdot \mathbf{v} &= -I_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Project $\mathbf{v}$ onto the Gradient Direction**

Divide both sides by the gradient magnitude $\Vert \nabla I \Vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$:

$$\frac{\nabla I}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert} \cdot \mathbf{v} \;=\; -\frac{I_t}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the Observable Quantity**

The left-hand side is the dot product of the **unit gradient** $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ with the true velocity $\mathbf{v}$. This quantity is called the **normal flow**:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_n \;&\equiv\; \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \mathbf{v} \\[4pt]
v_n \;&=\; -\frac{I_t}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the Unobservable Component**

The true velocity can be decomposed into a component along $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ plus a component along the orthogonal (tangential) direction $\hat{\mathbf{t}}$:

$$\mathbf{v} \;=\; v_n \hat{\mathbf{n}} + v_t \hat{\mathbf{t}}$$

The tangential component $v_t$ is **multiplied by zero** in the OFCE (because $\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{t}} = 0$), so the constraint equation *erases* any information about $v_t$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \mathbf{v} \;=\; \hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot (v_n \hat{\mathbf{n}} + v_t \hat{\mathbf{t}}) \;=\; v_n (\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}) + v_t (\hat{\mathbf{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{t}}) \;=\; v_n$$

This is the formal statement of the **Aperture Problem**: infinitely many velocities $\mathbf{v}$ along the *constraint line* in velocity space satisfy the local observation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Solutions to the Aperture Problem

| Method | Family | Core Idea | Aperture Resolution Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lucas–Kanade** | Local gradient | Solves OFCE in least-squares sense over window $\Omega$ | Assumes $\mathbf{v}$ is *constant* within $\Omega$ → over-constrains the system via $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$ |
| **Horn–Schunck** | Global variational | Adds smoothness penalty $\lambda \Vert \nabla \mathbf{v} \Vert^2$ | Spreads motion information from textured regions into ambiguous ones |
| **Black–Anandan** | Robust estimation | Uses a Lorentzian penalty to handle flow outliers | Robust error norm tolerates the OFCE violation at edges |
| **Brox et al.** | Combined | Warping + gradient constancy + smoothness | Multi-cue reduces aperture-induced bias |
| **Multi-aperture / Correlation** | Feature-based | Matches patches *between* frames | Larger effective aperture resolves tangential ambiguity |
| **Structure Tensor (second-moment)** | Corner detection | Verifies two large eigenvalues in $\sum \nabla I \nabla I^{\top}$ | Rejects aperture-prone regions (single eigenvalue) |
| **Deep learning (FlowNet, RAFT)** | Data-driven | Learns to infer full flow end-to-end | Implicit global context resolves the ambiguity |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A horizontal edge moves such that $I_x = 50$, $I_y = 0$, $I_t = -100$ in a $5 \times 5$ aperture. Compute the normal flow.

**Solution:**

$$\Vert \nabla I \Vert \;=\; \sqrt{50^2 + 0^2} \;=\; 50$$

$$v_n \;=\; -\frac{I_t}{\Vert \nabla I \Vert} \;=\; -\frac{(-100)}{50} \;=\; +2.0 \text{ pixels/frame}$$

**Interpretation:** The edge is moving *towards increasing $x$* at 2 pixels/frame *in the normal direction* (which, for a horizontal edge, is also the horizontal direction). However, if the same edge were a *vertical* edge ($I_x = 0, I_y = 50$), the same $I_t$ would yield the same $v_n$ but now the *tangential* direction would be horizontal — and we would *not* be able to tell whether the edge is moving horizontally or merely shifting along its own length.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Aperture Problem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Image Frame I x y t] --> B[Local Aperture Window Omega]
    B --> C[Compute Spatial Gradients Ix and Iy]
    C --> D[Compute Temporal Gradient It]
    D --> E[Form Optical Flow Constraint Equation]
    E --> F{Single Equation Two Unknowns u v}
    F --> G[Project onto Gradient Direction]
    G --> H[Extract Normal Flow vn = minus It divided by norm gradI]
    H --> I[Observable Component]
    F --> J[Tangential Component vt]
    J --> K[Unobservable Locally]
    J --> L[This is the Aperture Problem]
    I --> M[Resolve via Lucas Kanade Window]
    I --> N[Resolve via Horn Schunck Smoothness]
    I --> O[Resolve via Multi Aperture Correlation]
    I --> P[Resolve via Deep Learning Context]
    M --> Q[Full 2D Optical Flow Output]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constraint Line in Velocity Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[True Velocity v at point P] --> B[Decompose into Normal plus Tangential]
    B --> C[Normal Component vn along grad I]
    B --> D[Tangential Component vt along edge]
    C --> E[Observed through aperture]
    D --> F[Hidden by aperture]
    E --> G[Constraint Line in u v plane]
    F --> G
    G --> H[All velocities on this line are consistent with local observation]
    H --> I[Only global context disambiguates]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Comparison of Resolution Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Aperture Problem Detected] --> Branch{Resolution Strategy}
    Branch --> Local[Lucas Kanade Local Window]
    Branch --> Global[Horn Schunck Global Smoothness]
    Branch --> Feature[Multi Aperture Feature Matching]
    Branch --> Learning[End to End Deep Networks]
    Local --> LocalNote[Assumes constant flow in window]
    Global --> GlobalNote[Adds Laplacian smoothness penalty]
    Feature --> FeatureNote[Matches larger patches across frames]
    Learning --> LearningNote[Learns implicit global context from data]
```
