# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse the Essential Matrix $\mathbf{E}$ with the Fundamental Matrix $\mathbf{F}$**: $\mathbf{E}$ is for *calibrated* cameras (normalized coordinates); $\mathbf{F}$ is for *uncalibrated* (pixel coordinates). Mixing them loses **3 marks instantly**.
> - **In LK derivations**, students often forget to multiply by **$\Delta t$** before the Taylor expansion, or divide incorrectly at the end. Show *every* step.
> - **In SfM triangulation**, ensure the homogeneous-coordinate **DMin trick** is applied correctly. A missing $\mathbf{X}[3]/=1$ normalization costs 2 marks.
> - **Lucas-Kanade assumes small motion** — if the question says "large displacement", you **must** mention **Pyramidal LK**.
> - **Bundle Adjustment is non-linear** — students often write it as a linear least-squares problem and lose 3 marks. Mention **Levenberg-Marquardt**.

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Brightness Constancy Assumption and derive the Optical Flow Constraint Equation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Brightness Constancy Assumption (BCA) states that the intensity of an image pixel corresponding to a physical point in the scene remains constant across consecutive frames despite motion.

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t,\ y + v\Delta t,\ t + \Delta t)$$

Applying a first-order Taylor series expansion to the RHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x + u\Delta t,\ y + v\Delta t,\ t + \Delta t) \approx & I(x,y,t) \\
& + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} u\Delta t \\
& + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y} v\Delta t \\
& + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating with LHS via BCA and dividing by $\Delta t$:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation**. *(3 marks: 1 for assumption, 2 for derivation)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Aperture Problem? How does Lucas-Kanade overcome it?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Aperture Problem refers to the ambiguity that arises when local image measurements (such as gradient information within a small window) can only constrain the optical flow component **perpendicular to the local intensity gradient** (the *normal flow*), not the true flow direction. A single pixel or a uniform edge gives one equation but two unknowns, leaving the tangential flow component indeterminate.

Lucas-Kanade overcomes this by imposing the **Spatial Coherence Constraint**, which assumes all pixels in a local $W \times W$ window share the same flow vector $(u, v)$. This converts the under-determined single equation into an **over-determined system** solvable via least squares, provided the window contains a *corner-like* region (non-singular structure tensor). *(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Lucas-Kanade optical flow equation from the brightness constancy assumption. Explain the role of the structure tensor and discuss its connection to the Aperture Problem and Harris Corner detection.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Brightness Constancy Assumption [1 mark]:**
$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + u\Delta t,\ y + v\Delta t,\ t + \Delta t)$$

**(ii) Taylor Expansion [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x + u\Delta t,\ y + v\Delta t,\ t + \Delta t) \approx & I(x,y,t) + I_x u\Delta t \\
& + I_y v\Delta t + I_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting and dividing by $\Delta t$:

$$I_x u + I_y v = -I_t$$

**(iii) Spatial Coherence Setup [2 marks]:**
Stacking equations for all pixels in window $\Omega$ gives $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is $n \times 2$ and $n = W^2$.

**(iv) Least-Squares Solution [1 mark]:**

$$\mathbf{u} = -(\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

**(v) Structure Tensor & Connection to Aperture Problem [1 mark]:**
The matrix $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}$ is the structure tensor. If $\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{M}) \approx 0$ (e.g., on a flat or edge region), the inverse does not exist — this is exactly the *Aperture Problem*. The Harris Corner Detector uses the same eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ to classify corners (both large), edges (one large), and flat regions (both small).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Pyramidal Lucas-Kanade algorithm. Why is it required? Show its iterative refinement at a single level.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Motivation [1 mark]:**
Standard LK assumes **|displacement| < 1 pixel** because it uses a first-order Taylor approximation. For large motions, the linearization breaks down.

**(ii) Image Pyramid Construction [2 marks]:**
Build Gaussian pyramid: $I^0 = I$, and for level $\ell = 1, \dots, L-1$, apply low-pass filtering and downsampling by 2. At coarse level $L-1$, large pixel motions in the original image become **sub-pixel motions** that satisfy LK's small-motion assumption.

**(iii) Coarse-to-Fine Estimation [2 marks]:**
At level $L-1$, compute initial flow $\mathbf{u}^{L-1}$. Propagate to finer level $\ell$ as an initial guess $\mathbf{u}^{\ell}_0 = 2 \cdot \mathbf{u}^{\ell+1}$.

**(iv) Iterative Refinement at Level $\ell$ [2 marks]:**
At each iteration $k$, warp image $I_2^{\ell}$ by current estimate $\mathbf{u}^{\ell}_k$, compute residual:

$$I_t^{\ell}(x, y) = I_2^{\ell}(x + u^{\ell}_k, y + v^{\ell}_k) - I_1^{\ell}(x, y)$$

Solve the LK system on the residual:

$$\Delta \mathbf{u} = -(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$$

Update:

$$\mathbf{u}^{\ell}_{k+1} = \mathbf{u}^{\ell}_k + \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

Converge when $\vert \Delta \mathbf{u} \vert < \epsilon$. Propagate result to the next finer level.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Structure from Motion (SfM) pipeline. Discuss the role of feature detection, matching, and the Fundamental Matrix in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SfM Definition [1 mark]:**
SfM is the recovery of 3D scene structure and camera motion from a sequence of 2D images.

**(ii) Feature Detection [2 marks]:**
Use scale- and rotation-invariant detectors such as **SIFT**, **SURF**, or **ORB**. These extract distinctive keypoints that can be reliably matched across views despite changes in scale, rotation, and illumination.

**(iii) Feature Matching [1 mark]:**
Match keypoints between image pairs using descriptor similarity (e.g., L2 norm for SIFT, Hamming distance for ORB). Apply Lowe's ratio test to filter ambiguous matches.

**(iv) Fundamental Matrix Estimation [2 marks]:**
With at least **8 point correspondences**, estimate the Fundamental Matrix $\mathbf{F}$ using the **8-point algorithm**:

$$\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0$$

The linear system is $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} = \mathbf{0}$ where $\mathbf{f}$ is the 9-vectorized $\mathbf{F}$, and the solution is the right singular vector of $\mathbf{A}$ corresponding to the smallest singular value. The result is then **rank-2 enforced** via SVD by setting the smallest singular value to zero.

**(v) Pose Recovery [1 mark]:**
From $\mathbf{F}$ (or $\mathbf{E}$ if calibrated), decompose $\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{U} \text{diag}(1,1,0) \mathbf{V}^T$ into four possible $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ pairs; disambiguate via chirality (points must be in front of both cameras).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is Bundle Adjustment? Derive the cost function and explain how it is optimized. Why is it called the "gold standard" of SfM?**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition [1 mark]:**
Bundle Adjustment (BA) is the joint non-linear refinement of all camera poses $\{\mathbf{P}_j\}_{j=1}^{N_c}$ and all 3D scene points $\{\mathbf{X}_i\}_{i=1}^{N_p}$ to minimize the total reprojection error across all observations.

**(ii) Cost Function [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\{\mathbf{P}_j\}, \{\mathbf{X}_i\}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \sum_{j=1}^{N_c} \rho\left( \Vert \mathbf{x}_{ij} - \pi(\mathbf{P}_j \mathbf{X}_i) \Vert^2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\pi(\cdot)$ is the projection function and $\rho$ is a robust loss (Huber, Cauchy) to down-weight outliers.

**(iii) Optimization via Levenberg-Marquardt [2 marks]:**
The cost is non-linear in $\mathbf{R}$ (so parameterize via rotation vectors or quaternions). Linearize via Taylor expansion to get normal equations:

$$(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J} + \lambda \text{diag}(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J})) \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = -\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{r}$$

Iteratively update parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} + \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$, where $\mathbf{J}$ is the Jacobian of residuals and $\lambda$ is the LM damping parameter (trust-region radius).

**(iv) Why "Gold Standard" [2 marks]:**
BA provides the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate** under isotropic Gaussian reprojection noise. It simultaneously corrects drift accumulated during sequential SfM and optimally distributes error across all observations. Modern libraries like **Ceres Solver**, **g2o**, and **GTSAM** exploit the **sparse block structure** of $\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{J}$ for tractability even with millions of points.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly)**

- **Brightness Constancy Assumption (BCA):** Intensity of a moving point is invariant over short time. Foundation of differential optical flow.
- **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE):** $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$. One equation, two unknowns → under-determined.
- **Aperture Problem:** Local observations cannot resolve the *normal* component ambiguity on edges. Solved by Spatial Coherence.
- **Lucas-Kanade Window:** Local $W \times W$ patch sharing the same $(u, v)$; uses **weighted least squares**.
- **Structure Tensor:** $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}$. Invertibility $\Leftrightarrow$ corner region $\Leftrightarrow$ reliable LK flow.
- **Pyramidal LK:** Multi-resolution extension that handles motions > 1 pixel. Coarse-to-fine propagation.
- **Camera Projection:** $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$. $\mathbf{K}$ intrinsic, $[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]$ extrinsic.
- **Essential vs Fundamental Matrix:** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ (calibrated); $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-T}\mathbf{E}\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ (uncalibrated).
- **Epipolar Constraint:** $\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0$. Encodes the geometry of two-view observations.
- **8-Point Algorithm:** Minimum solver for $\mathbf{F}$. Requires $\geq 8$ correspondences; enforce rank-2 via SVD.
- **Triangulation:** Recover 3D point $\mathbf{X}$ from 2D correspondences and camera matrices.
- **Bundle Adjustment:** Joint non-linear optimization of all cameras and points via **Levenberg-Marquardt**. Gold-standard refinement.
- **SfM vs Stereo:** Stereo uses *fixed, known* baseline; SfM uses *unknown* camera motion recovered from images.
- **Always remember** in KTU exam: Show the **Taylor expansion** explicitly, label **structure tensor**, and mention **LM solver** for BA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scene Analysis - Examples of Real Time Applications, In-Vehicle Vision System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT833 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scene Analysis - Examples of Real Time Applications, In-Vehicle Vision System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Scene Analysis

**Scene Analysis** is the high-level stage of computer vision that moves beyond pixel-level and object-level processing to perform *semantic interpretation* of an entire image or video frame. It integrates detection, recognition, segmentation, tracking, spatial reasoning, and contextual inference to answer the question: *"What is happening, where, and what will happen next?"*

According to the KTU 2024 OEEVT833 syllabus, scene analysis encompasses the unified interpretation of multi-object relationships, activity recognition, 3D layout estimation, and event prediction in a given visual scene. It is the cognitive "brain" of any vision pipeline, transforming raw detections into actionable intelligence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *Scene Analysis involves interpreting the content of an image or image sequence to derive a meaningful description of the scene, including object recognition, spatial relationships, and event understanding.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Brain Inside the Eyes"

Imagine a baby looking at a busy street. The eyes (cameras) capture light, but it is the *brain* (scene analysis module) that tells the baby:
- "There is a car approaching fast" (object detection + motion)
- "The ball is rolling toward the road" (object + trajectory prediction)
- "A pedestrian is crossing" (recognition + activity)

**Scene analysis is that brain.** It fuses *what* is in the scene (objects), *where* they are (spatial layout), *how they move* (dynamics), and *what it means* (semantics) into a coherent narrative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time Constraint**: For applications like autonomous driving, the analysis must complete within **≤ 100 ms** (often **≤ 33 ms** for 30 FPS, **≤ 16.7 ms** for 60 FPS). Any latency beyond this threshold is treated as a system failure.

### 1.3 Real-Time Applications — The Big Picture

Real-time computer vision systems must process incoming video frames and produce results **faster than, or at the same rate as, frame acquisition**. The KTU 2024 module highlights the following canonical domains:

| Domain | Application Example | Critical Latency |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Lane keeping, collision avoidance | **≤ 50 ms** |
| Surveillance | Intrusion detection, crowd monitoring | **≤ 200 ms** |
| Medical | Endoscopic tool tracking, surgery assistance | **≤ 100 ms** |
| Industrial | Defect detection on conveyor belts | **≤ 33 ms** |
| Sports | Ball tracking, player pose estimation | **≤ 20 ms** |
| Robotics | Grasping, SLAM, obstacle avoidance | **≤ 30 ms** |

### 1.4 In-Vehicle Vision System — Definition

An **In-Vehicle Vision System (IVVS)**, also known as an **On-Board Vision System**, is a specialized real-time embedded computer vision platform mounted within a vehicle. It uses cameras (mono, stereo, fisheye, infrared, RGB-D) combined with machine-learning algorithms to perceive the driving environment and assist or automate driving decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — ADAS**: In-Vehicle Vision Systems form the perceptual backbone of **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)** and **autonomous driving stacks** (SAE Levels 1–5).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Pipeline of Scene Analysis

Scene analysis is not a single algorithm — it is a **multi-stage pipeline** where each stage builds context for the next. The KTU 2024 model classifies this into five functional layers:

**Layer 1 — Pre-Processing (Low-Level Vision)**
- Image acquisition (camera frame grab at **30–60 FPS**)
- Noise filtering (Gaussian, bilateral, median)
- Color space conversion (RGB → HSV, RGB → Grayscale)
- Geometric correction (lens distortion removal using intrinsic matrix **K**)
- Histogram equalization for illumination normalization

**Layer 2 — Feature Extraction (Mid-Level Vision)**
- Edge detection (Canny, Sobel)
- Corner/Keypoint detection (Harris, SIFT, ORB, SURF)
- Texture descriptors (LBP, GLCM, HOG)
- Deep feature embeddings (CNN backbone — ResNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet)

**Layer 3 — Object Detection & Recognition**
- Traditional: Viola–Jones (Haar cascades), HOG + SVM
- Deep Learning: YOLO (v5/v8), SSD, Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, DETR
- Output: bounding boxes $\mathbf{B} = (x, y, w, h, \text{class}, \text{confidence})$

**Layer 4 — Semantic & Spatial Reasoning**
- Semantic segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab, SegFormer)
- Instance segmentation (Mask R-CNN)
- Panoptic segmentation (Panoptic FPN)
- Depth estimation (monocular or stereo)
- Optical flow (Lucas–Kanade, Farnebäck, FlowNet)

**Layer 5 — Scene Understanding & Decision**
- Multi-object tracking (SORT, DeepSORT, ByteTrack)
- Behavior prediction (LSTM, Transformers, Social GAN)
- Scene graph generation (objects + relationships)
- Decision output: brake, steer, alert, label

### 2.2 Core Algorithmic Theories

**A. Object Detection — Anchor-Based Formulation**
Modern detectors predict offsets $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta w, \Delta h)$ relative to predefined anchor boxes:
$$
b_x = \sigma(t_x) + c_x, \quad b_y = \sigma(t_y) + c_y
$$
$$
b_w = p_w \, e^{t_w}, \quad b_h = p_h \, e^{t_h}
$$
where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the sigmoid activation, $c_x, c_y$ are grid offsets, and $p_w, p_h$ are anchor dimensions.

**B. Tracking-by-Detection — Kalman Filter State**
The state vector at time $t$:
$$
\mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} u_t \\ v_t \\ s_t \\ r_t \\ \dot{u}_t \\ \dot{v}_t \\ \dot{s}_t \end{bmatrix}
$$
with prediction:
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1}
$$
and update:
$$
\mathbf{x}_{t \vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} + \mathbf{K}_t \left( \mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} \right)
$$

**C. Optical Flow Constraint (Lucas–Kanade)**
$$
I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0
$$
solved over a local neighborhood via least squares:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum I_x^2 & \sum I_x I_y \\ \sum I_x I_y & \sum I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} -\sum I_x I_t \\ -\sum I_y I_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

**D. Lane Detection — Polynomial Fit**
Lanes are typically modeled as:
$$
x = a \, y^2 + b \, y + c
$$
fitted by RANSAC over edge pixels detected by Canny + Hough Transform.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Use Case | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IoU** (Intersection over Union) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | Detection accuracy | dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | False-positive sensitivity | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Miss-rate sensitivity | dimensionless |
| 4 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Balanced metric | dimensionless |
| 5 | **mAP** (mean Average Precision) | $\text{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{AP}_i$ | Multi-class detection | dimensionless |
| 6 | **FPS** (Frames Per Second) | $\text{FPS} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}}$ | Throughput | frames/sec |
| 7 | **Latency** | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{acquire}} + T_{\text{infer}} + T_{\text{decide}}$ | Real-time bound | ms |
| 8 | **Time-to-Collision** | $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{d}{\dot{d}}$ | Forward collision warning | seconds |
| 9 | **Optical Flow** | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | Motion estimation | px/frame |
| 10 | **Pin-hole Camera** | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \, \vert \, \mathbf{t}] \, \mathbf{X}$ | 3D → 2D projection | px |
| 11 | **Lane Polynomial** | $x = a y^2 + b y + c$ | Ego-lane estimation | px |
| 12 | **Kalman Predict** | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_{t-1 \vert t-1}$ | Object tracking | state vector |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** (Tesla, Toyota, Bosch, Mobileye) deploy scene-analysis stacks on custom SoCs (e.g., Tesla HW3/HW4, NVIDIA Orin, Qualcomm Ride).
- **Tier-1 Suppliers** use these algorithms for **NCAP 2026** safety ratings (AEB, LKA, ELKS).
- **Smart Cities** rely on scene analysis for **traffic flow optimization** and **incident detection**.
- **Healthcare AI** uses it for **intra-operative guidance** (e.g., da Vinci Surgical System).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Intersection over Union (IoU)

IoU is the **single most important evaluation metric** in modern object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR). Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the bounding boxes**
Let $B_p = (x_p^{\min}, y_p^{\min}, x_p^{\max}, y_p^{\max})$ be the *predicted* box and $B_{gt} = (x_{gt}^{\min}, y_{gt}^{\min}, x_{gt}^{\max}, y_{gt}^{\max})$ be the *ground-truth* box.

**Step 2 — Compute the coordinates of the intersection rectangle**
$$
x_{\text{int}}^{\min} = \max(x_p^{\min}, x_{gt}^{\min})
$$
$$
y_{\text{int}}^{\min} = \max(y_p^{\min}, y_{gt}^{\min})
$$
$$
x_{\text{int}}^{\max} = \min(x_p^{\max}, x_{gt}^{\max})
$$
$$
y_{\text{int}}^{\max} = \min(y_p^{\max}, y_{gt}^{\max})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intersection area**
$$
\text{Area}_{\text{int}} = \max\!\left(0,\; x_{\text{int}}^{\max} - x_{\text{int}}^{\min}\right) \cdot \max\!\left(0,\; y_{\text{int}}^{\max} - y_{\text{int}}^{\min}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the union area**
$$
\text{Area}_{\text{union}} = \text{Area}_{B_p} + \text{Area}_{B_{gt}} - \text{Area}_{\text{int}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final IoU ratio**
$$
\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Area}_{\text{int}}}{\text{Area}_{\text{union}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked numerical example**
Suppose $B_p = (10, 10, 50, 50)$ and $B_{gt} = (30, 30, 70, 70)$.

Intersection corners: $(30, 30)$ to $(50, 50)$. So $\text{Area}_{\text{int}} = 20 \times 20 = 400$.

$$
\text{Area}_{B_p} = 40 \times 40 = 1600
$$
$$
\text{Area}_{B_{gt}} = 40 \times 40 = 1600
$$
$$
\text{Area}_{\text{union}} = 1600 + 1600 - 400 = 2800
$$
$$
\text{IoU} = \dfrac{400}{2800} = \dfrac{1}{7} \approx 0.143
$$

For a detection to count as a *True Positive* under standard PASCAL VOC rules, **IoU ≥ 0.5**.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Time-to-Collision (TTC)

TTC is the cornerstone of **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** and **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)**.

**Step 1 — Setup**
A vehicle travels at relative velocity $\dot{d}$ (rate of change of distance to obstacle $d$). Both are measured by stereo cameras or radar.

**Step 2 — Continuous-time derivation**
$$
\text{TTC}(t) = \dfrac{d(t)}{\dot{d}(t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Discrete-time formulation (frame $t$ to $t+1$)**
$$
\dot{d}_t = \dfrac{d_{t-1} - d_t}{\Delta t}, \quad \Delta t = \dfrac{1}{\text{FPS}}
$$
$$
\text{TTC}_t = \dfrac{d_t \cdot \Delta t}{d_{t-1} - d_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision thresholds (Euro NCAP)**
- TTC $\leq$ **2.7 s** → warning issued
- TTC $\leq$ **1.4 s** → emergency braking initiated

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ADAS Functions vs. Algorithms

| ADAS Function | Scene Analysis Task | Algorithm Used | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)** | Lane marking detection | Canny + Hough / SCNN / Ultra Fast Lane Detection | Lane offset (m) |
| **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** | Lane curve + ego position | Polynomial fit $x = ay^2 + by + c$ | Steering angle |
| **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** | Vehicle detection + ranging | YOLO + stereo depth | TTC (s) |
| **Pedestrian Detection (PD)** | Person detection in path | YOLOv8, EfficientDet, CSP | Bounding box + distance |
| **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)** | Sign classification | CNN classifier (GTSRB dataset) | Sign ID + confidence |
| **Driver Drowsiness Detection (DDD)** | Eye/face landmark analysis | MediaPipe + EAR (Eye Aspect Ratio) | Alert state |
| **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** | Side-rear vehicle tracking | SORT / DeepSORT | Side occupancy flag |
| **Surround View Monitoring (SVM)** | Multi-camera stitching | Homography + bird's-eye warp | Top-down image |
| **Parking Assist** | Free-slot detection | Bird's-eye + semantic segmentation | Slot coordinates |

### 3.4 Performance Trade-off Curve (FPS vs. mAP)

For the KTU board exam, students must remember that real-time detectors lie on a Pareto frontier:

| Model | Backbone | mAP@0.5 | FPS (Tesla V100) | Real-time? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| YOLOv8-n | CSPDarknet-nano | 37.3 | **> 500** | ✅ Embedded |
| YOLOv8-s | CSPDarknet-small | 44.9 | ~300 | ✅ |
| YOLOv8-m | CSPDarknet-medium | 50.2 | ~150 | ✅ Mid-range |
| Faster R-CNN | ResNet-50 | 47.0 | ~25 | ❌ Cloud only |
| DETR | ResNet-50 | 42.0 | ~28 | ❌ |
| RT-DETR | ResNet-50 | 53.1 | ~108 | ✅ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU board)**: An embedded automotive system must achieve **mAP@0.5 ≥ 40** and **FPS ≥ 30** simultaneously to qualify as a real-time detector.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End In-Vehicle Vision System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensors["PERCEPTION SENSORS"]
        CamF["Front RGB Camera 1280x960 @ 60FPS"]
        CamR["Rear Camera"]
        CamL["Left Fisheye Camera"]
        CamRR["Right Fisheye Camera"]
        Stereo["Stereo Camera Pair"]
        IR["IR Driver Monitor Camera"]
        Radar["77 GHz Radar"]
        Lidar["LiDAR 360 deg"]
    end

    subgraph PreProc["PRE-PROCESSING UNIT FPGA"]
        ISP["Image Signal Processor"]
        Dewarp["Lens Dewarping"]
        Sync["Time Synchronization PTP"]
        Stitch["Multi-Camera Stitching"]
    end

    subgraph CVStack["AI VISION STACK GPU SoC"]
        Det["Object Detector YOLO v8"]
        Seg["Semantic Segmentation SegFormer"]
        Lane["Lane Detector UFLD"]
        Sign["Traffic Sign Classifier"]
        Driver["Drowsiness Detection EAR + Pose"]
        Track["Multi-Object Tracker ByteTrack"]
        Flow["Optical Flow FlowNet"]
        Depth["Stereo Depth Estimation"]
    end

    subgraph Fusion["SENSOR FUSION ECU"]
        FUSE["Kalman + Bayesian Fusion"]
        TTC["Time-to-Collision Estimator"]
        OCC["Occupancy Grid Builder"]
    end

    subgraph Decision["DECISION AND CONTROL UNIT"]
        Plan["Behavior Planner"]
        Ctrl["Vehicle Controller ACC AEB LKA"]
        HMI["Driver HMI Alerts"]
        V2X["V2X Communicator"]
    end

    CamF --> ISP
    CamR --> ISP
    CamL --> Dewarp
    CamRR --> Dewarp
    Stereo --> ISP
    IR --> ISP
    Radar --> FUSE
    Lidar --> FUSE

    ISP --> Sync
    Dewarp --> Stitch
    Sync --> Stitch
    Stitch --> Det
    Stitch --> Seg
    Stitch --> Lane
    Stitch --> Sign
    IR --> Driver

    Det --> Track
    Seg --> OCC
    Lane --> Plan
    Sign --> Plan
    Driver --> HMI
    Track --> FUSE
    Depth --> TTC
    Flow --> Plan

    FUSE --> TTC
    TTC --> Plan
    OCC --> Plan
    Plan --> Ctrl
    Plan --> HMI
    Plan --> V2X
```

### 4.2 Scene Analysis — Hierarchical Reasoning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Image Frame 1920x1080"] --> B["Low Level Pre-Process"]
    B --> C["Feature Extraction CNN"]
    C --> D["Object Detection YOLO"]
    C --> E["Segmentation U-Net"]
    C --> F["Depth Estimation"]
    D --> G["Tracker SORT"]
    E --> H["Scene Graph Builder"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Activity Reasoning LSTM"]
    I --> J["Scene Description JSON"]
    J --> K["Decision Engine"]
```

### 4.3 Driver Drowsiness Detection Sub-Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Capture Driver Frame 30FPS"] --> P2["Face Detection RetinaFace"]
    P2 --> P3["68 Point Landmark"]
    P3 --> P4["Eye Landmark Extraction"]
    P4 --> P5["EAR Computation"]
    P5 --> P6{"EAR less than 0.21 for 20 frames"}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7["Drowsy Alert HMI Sound Seat Vibration"]
    P6 -- No --> P8["Monitor Continues"]
    P3 --> P9["Head Pose Estimation PnP"]
    P9 --> P10{"Yaw greater than 30 deg"}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11["Distraction Warning"]
    P7 --> P12["Log Event to Blackbox"]
    P11 --> P12
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define scene analysis in computer vision. List any four real-time applications.*

**Model Answer:**
Scene analysis is the high-level interpretation stage of a computer vision system that integrates object detection, recognition, spatial reasoning, and contextual inference to derive a semantic description of an image or video sequence.

Four real-time applications:
1. **Autonomous driving / ADAS** — lane detection, collision avoidance.
2. **Video surveillance** — intrusion detection, crowd density estimation.
3. **Medical imaging** — endoscopic tool tracking, surgical assistance.
4. **Industrial inspection** — surface defect detection on production lines.

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is an in-vehicle vision system? Mention any two functions it performs.*

**Model Answer:**
An in-vehicle vision system (IVVS) is an embedded real-time computer vision platform mounted within a vehicle that uses cameras and machine-learning algorithms to perceive the surrounding environment and assist or automate driving decisions.

Two functions:
1. **Lane Detection and Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** — identifies lane markings and issues steering corrections.
2. **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** — detects vehicles ahead and computes Time-to-Collision (TTC) for automatic emergency braking.

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for functions)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose 4–5 marks in Part B questions by:
> 1. Writing only the **algorithm name** (e.g., "YOLO") without explaining **inputs, outputs, loss function, and architecture**.
> 2. Skipping the **numerical example** — KTU examiners reward at least **one worked calculation** (IoU, TTC, EAR, etc.).
> 3. Confusing **ADAS Level 1 / Level 2 / Level 3** with SAE automation levels — this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. Not drawing a **block diagram** — a well-labeled architecture flow earns **easy 3 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an in-vehicle vision system with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the lane detection algorithm used in ADAS. Derive the lane polynomial model and explain how RANSAC improves robustness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The architecture of an in-vehicle vision system is organized into four hierarchical layers:

**1. Sensor Layer** — Captures raw visual input:
- **Front-facing RGB camera** at **1280 × 960 resolution** and **60 FPS**.
- **Stereo camera pair** for depth estimation (baseline typically **120 mm**).
- **Fisheye / surround-view cameras** for blind-spot coverage.
- **IR camera** for driver monitoring (illumination-invariant).
- **Radar and LiDAR** as complementary sensors (camera alone fails in fog/glare).

**2. Pre-Processing Layer** (FPGA):
- Image Signal Processor (ISP): demosaicing, white balance.
- Lens dewarping to remove fisheye distortion using intrinsic matrix **K**.
- PTP time synchronization across sensors to within **1 ms**.

**3. AI Vision Stack** (GPU / NPU SoC such as **NVIDIA Orin**):
- Object detection (YOLOv8)
- Semantic segmentation (SegFormer)
- Lane detection (UFLD)
- Traffic sign classification
- Driver monitoring (EAR-based)
- Multi-object tracking (ByteTrack)

**4. Decision and Control Layer**:
- Behavior planner → Vehicle controller (ACC, AEB, LKA)
- HMI alerts (visual + auditory + haptic seat vibration)
- V2X communication (IEEE 802.11p / C-V2X)

*Block diagram as drawn in SECTION_4.1 carries 3 marks.*

*(7 marks distributed: 1 sensor, 1 preproc, 2 AI stack, 1 decision, 2 diagram)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Image Pre-Processing**
Convert to grayscale, apply **Gaussian blur** (kernel = **5 × 5**, σ = **1.5**), then **Canny edge detection** with thresholds (low = **50**, high = **150**).

**Step 2 — Region of Interest (ROI) Masking**
Apply a trapezoidal mask covering the lower-half of the frame where lane markings lie.

**Step 3 — Hough Line Transform**
For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$, map to Hough space:
$$
\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta
$$
Yielding a set of line segments $(\rho, \theta)$.

**Step 4 — Line Filtering and Grouping**
Cluster lines by slope sign (positive = left lane, negative = right lane) and reject outliers using **RANSAC**.

**Step 5 — Polynomial Fit**
Each lane boundary is fit as:
$$
x = a \, y^2 + b \, y + c
$$
Using **least squares** over surviving RANSAC inliers:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sum y_i^4 & \sum y_i^3 & \sum y_i^2 \\ \sum y_i^3 & \sum y_i^2 & \sum y_i \\ \sum y_i^2 & \sum y_i & N \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum x_i y_i^2 \\ \sum x_i y_i \\ \sum x_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Why RANSAC?** Road scenes contain spurious edges (shadows, road patches, vehicles). Standard least-squares is **sensitive to outliers**, while RANSAC repeatedly samples minimal subsets, fits the model, and retains the fit with the **maximum inlier count**. This improves lane detection robustness by **> 30%** in noisy urban scenes.

**Output**: Polynomial coefficients $(a, b, c)$ for left and right lane, from which the lane center, curvature radius, and lateral offset are computed.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 pipeline, 2 derivation, 2 RANSAC justification, 1 diagram)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the working of a YOLO-based real-time object detector used in autonomous vehicles. Discuss its input-output format and loss function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forward-facing stereo camera detects a vehicle at distance $d_t = 35$ m in frame $t$ and $d_{t-1} = 38$ m in frame $t-1$ at $\text{FPS} = 25$. Compute Time-to-Collision (TTC) and state which ADAS action is triggered per Euro NCAP rules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**YOLO (You Only Look Once)** is a single-stage, real-time object detector that treats detection as a **single regression problem** from image pixels to bounding box coordinates and class probabilities.

**Input**: Image resized to **640 × 640** (YOLOv8 default).
**Backbone**: CSPDarknet for feature extraction at strides **8, 16, 32**.
**Neck**: PANet (Path Aggregation Network) for multi-scale feature fusion.
**Head**: Three detection heads predicting at 80×80, 40×40, 20×20 grids.

**Output tensor per grid cell**:
$$
\mathbf{O} = [t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h, p_o, c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{80}]
$$
Total: **5 + 80 = 85** values per anchor (3 anchors per cell in YOLOv5, anchor-free in YOLOv8).

**Decoding**:
$$
b_x = 2\sigma(t_x) - 0.5 + c_x, \quad b_y = 2\sigma(t_y) - 0.5 + c_y
$$
$$
b_w = \frac{p_w (2\sigma(t_w))^2}{S}, \quad b_h = \frac{p_h (2\sigma(t_h))^2}{S}
$$
$$
P(\text{class}_i) = c_i \cdot \sigma(p_o)
$$

**Loss function (composite)**:
$$
\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{\text{box}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{CIoU}} + \lambda_{\text{obj}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{obj}} + \lambda_{\text{cls}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}
$$
where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CIoU}}$ is the **Complete IoU loss**:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CIoU}} = 1 - \text{IoU} + \frac{\rho^2(b, b^{gt})}{c^2} + \alpha v
$$
with $\rho$ = Euclidean center distance, $c$ = diagonal of smallest enclosing box, and $v$ = aspect-ratio consistency.

**Post-processing**: **NMS (Non-Max Suppression)** with IoU threshold **0.7** removes duplicate detections.

**Why YOLO for autonomous vehicles?**
- Single forward pass → **> 60 FPS** on embedded GPUs.
- Global context (whole image seen simultaneously) → lower false-positive rate than sliding-window detectors.
- End-to-end trainable, deployable on **NVIDIA Orin / Tesla HW4**.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 architecture, 2 input/output, 2 loss, 1 deployment)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given**:
- $d_{t-1} = 38$ m (previous frame)
- $d_t = 35$ m (current frame)
- $\text{FPS} = 25$ → $\Delta t = 1/25 = 0.04$ s

**Step 1 — Compute closing rate (relative velocity)**
$$
\dot{d}_t = \frac{d_{t-1} - d_t}{\Delta t} = \frac{38 - 35}{0.04} = \frac{3}{0.04} = 75 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(Conversion logic: 3 m closed in 0.04 s ⇒ 75 m/s closing speed, equivalent to 270 km/h, indicating highway scenario.)*

**Step 2 — Compute TTC**
$$
\text{TTC}_t = \frac{d_t}{\dot{d}_t} = \frac{35}{75} \approx 0.467 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Euro NCAP thresholds**

| Threshold | ADAS Action |
|---|---|
| TTC ≤ **2.7 s** | Forward Collision **Warning** (FCW) |
| TTC ≤ **1.4 s** | Forward Collision **Warning + Brake Pre-charge** |
| TTC ≤ **0.8 s** | **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** initiated |

Since **TTC = 0.467 s < 0.8 s**, the system triggers **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** with maximum deceleration (~**9 m/s²** for typical AEB tuning).

**Engineering note**: The deceleration required is
$$
a_{\text{req}} = \frac{\dot{d}^2}{2 d} = \frac{75^2}{2 \times 35} \approx 80.4 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$
which exceeds tire-road friction limits (~**8 m/s²** on dry asphalt), confirming the collision is **unavoidable** with braking alone — AEB will mitigate impact but cannot prevent it. This is a classical limit-case highlighting the need for **predictive ADAS** operating at TTC ≥ **3 s**.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 closing rate, 2 TTC computation, 2 threshold logic, 1 engineering insight)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Final Reading**

- ✅ **Scene Analysis Definition**: Semantic interpretation of entire image/frame → objects + spatial layout + activity + events.
- ✅ **Five-layer pipeline**: Pre-Processing → Feature Extraction → Detection → Segmentation → Scene Reasoning.
- ✅ **Real-time bound**: ≥ **30 FPS**, latency ≤ **33 ms** for general; ≤ **100 ms** for ADAS; ≤ **16.7 ms** for **60 FPS** sports/AR.
- ✅ **In-Vehicle Vision System (IVVS)**: Camera-based perception for ADAS; complementary to radar/LiDAR.
- ✅ **Core ADAS functions** (must memorize): LDW, LKA, FCW, AEB, BSD, TSR, ACC, PD, DDD, SVM.
- ✅ **Key metrics** (must memorize): IoU, mAP, Precision, Recall, F1, FPS, TTC, EAR.
- ✅ **YOLO output formula**: $b_x = 2\sigma(t_x) - 0.5 + c_x$ — frequently asked in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ **Lane model**: $x = ay^2 + by + c$ — solve using normal equations.
- ✅ **TTC thresholds (Euro NCAP)**: 2.7 s = warn, 1.4 s = pre-brake, 0.8 s = AEB.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter**: Predict → Update; state vector contains position, scale, ratio, velocities.
- ✅ **Driver Drowsiness**: EAR < **0.21** for **20 consecutive frames** triggers alert.
- ✅ **SAE Levels**: L0 (none), L1 (driver assist), L2 (partial automation), L3 (conditional), L4 (high), L5 (full).
- ✅ **Datasets to cite**: KITTI, BDD100K, Waymo Open, nuScenes, Cityscapes, GTSRB.
- ✅ **Embedded SoCs**: NVIDIA Orin, Tesla HW4, Qualcomm Ride, Mobileye EyeQ6.
- ✅ **RANSAC** is **must-mention** for any noise-robustness question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for ADAS — "Lanes Fast, Pedestrians Safe, Signs Known, Driver Awake"**: L (LDW/LKA), F (FCW/AEB), P (PD), S (TSR), D (DDD).
